topic: winter moana dips — cold water therapy

turns.content: have you heard of cold water therapy? one of my friends has started doing ocean dips in winter and she's trying to get me to join her and i'm intrigued but also terrified

Cold water therapy has had a real surge of interest in recent years. The basic idea is that deliberate exposure to cold water — usually below about 15°C — triggers a range of physiological responses that can benefit your mood, immune function, and recovery. There's genuine science behind some of it and a lot of hype around the rest.

What's drawing you to it — the health benefits, the mental reset aspect, or just the fact that your friend is doing it and you're curious?

honestly the mental reset. i already love the moana and i find being near the ocean grounding, it's where i go when i need to clear my head. so the idea of actually getting in the water in winter, feeling that shock and then the calm afterwards — that appeals to me. my friend says after the first minute her mind goes completely quiet and she feels amazing for the rest of the day. i want that eh

The mental clarity piece is probably the most consistently reported benefit. What happens physiologically is that the cold shock triggers a release of norepinephrine — a neurotransmitter that sharpens focus and elevates mood. It's essentially a natural, very intense reset button. The first minute is uncomfortable because your body is reacting to the cold, but once you settle past that initial shock, your nervous system shifts and many people describe exactly what your friend does — a profound quiet.

For someone who already has a relationship with the ocean and uses it for grounding, this could be a really natural extension. You're not fighting the water, you're deepening what's already there.

A few practical questions: where would you be dipping — a beach, a harbour? And can you swim comfortably?

i can swim fine, i grew up in the water. my friend goes to one of the beaches nearby and just walks in up to her shoulders, stays for two or three minutes, and gets out. she doesn't actually swim, it's more like standing in it and breathing. which sounds simple but the water temp here in winter gets down to about ten or eleven degrees and that is properly cold

Ten to eleven degrees is genuinely cold water — that's the range where the benefits are real but so are the risks if you don't approach it sensibly. Your friend's method of walking in to shoulder depth and standing rather than swimming is actually the safest way to do it because you stay in control and can walk out the moment you need to.

Here's what I'd recommend for getting started safely:

**First few times:**
- Go with your friend, never alone. Cold water can cause gasping and disorientation and you want someone there.
- Start with just 30-60 seconds. Don't try to match her two-three minutes on day one.
- Walk in slowly. The urge is to run in and get it over with, but gradual entry lets your body adjust and keeps the cold shock response manageable.
- Focus entirely on your breathing. Slow exhales through the mouth. The cold will make you want to gasp and hyperventilate — controlling that is the whole game.

**Gear:**
- Neoprene booties to protect your feet from cold and sharp rocks.
- A warm towel and dry clothes right on the beach ready to go.
- A thermos of something hot for immediately after.

**After you get out:**
- You'll feel incredible for about ten seconds and then the cold will really hit as your skin temperature drops. Get dry and dressed quickly. Shivering is normal and healthy — it's your body warming itself.

The biggest safety rule: **never stay in longer than feels right trying to prove something.** Two minutes in eleven-degree water is plenty. There's no trophy for suffering.

the breathing thing is what worries me most. i know about cold shock because we talk about it in water safety around little ones and it's no joke. but i also trust my body to adapt if i give it time. and i've got the breathing skills from birth work — i spend my whole life coaching women to breathe through intense physical experiences so i should be able to coach myself through thirty seconds of cold water eh

That's actually a brilliant connection. The breathwork you teach your mamas through labour contractions — slow, controlled, intentional breathing through an intense sensation that your body wants to fight — is almost exactly the same skill you need in cold water. The cold is a stressor, your body reacts, and your breath is the tool that tells your nervous system "we're okay, we're choosing this."

If you can guide someone through transition in labour, you can absolutely guide yourself through a winter ocean dip. The sensation passes in waves, just like contractions do. That first thirty seconds is the peak and then it eases.

You've also got the right mindset — you said you trust your body to adapt. That's important because the fear of cold water is often worse than the cold itself. People who go in expecting to suffer usually do. People who go in expecting to breathe and be present usually find something else entirely.

i like that — expecting to breathe and be present instead of expecting to suffer. i think that's the difference between torture and therapy eh. okay i'm going to text my friend and tell her i'm in. when's the best time of day to do it and how often should i go

Morning is generally best for a few reasons:
- Cortisol is naturally higher in the morning, which helps your body handle the cold stress
- The norepinephrine boost sets up your mood and focus for the rest of the day
- You get it done before your brain has time to talk you out of it

Frequency: start with once or twice a week. Your body needs time to adapt between dips, especially in the first few weeks. After a month, if you're enjoying it and handling it well, you could increase to most mornings. Some people do it daily but there's no evidence that more frequent is significantly better — consistency matters more than frequency.

One thing to watch for: if you're doing this on mornings when you've been up overnight for a birth, skip it. Sleep deprivation plus cold water stress is a combination your body doesn't need. Save the dips for the mornings when you've actually slept.

that's a really good point about the sleep thing, i wouldn't have thought of that. my friend mentioned she sometimes brings her kids along too and they play on the beach while she dips. do you think Maia would be okay to try it or is it adults only

Kids can do cold water dips but with extra caution. Children lose body heat faster than adults because of their higher surface-area-to-body-mass ratio, so the same water temperature that's challenging for you is more intense for a smaller body.

If Maia wanted to try it, I'd suggest:
- Let her watch you a few times first so she knows what to expect
- Keep her time much shorter — thirty seconds to a minute maximum
- Don't push it if she doesn't want to go in. Watching from the beach with a hot chocolate is perfectly fine
- Make sure she has warm dry clothes to change into immediately after

But honestly, just having her on the beach while you dip could be a lovely morning ritual for the two of you even if she never gets in the water. Her being there, seeing her mum do something brave and intentional — that teaches her something about self-care whether she joins you or not.

i love that idea actually. she'd probably want to watch a few times and then she'd be straight in because that's how she is — cautious observer and then all in. she's braver than me in a lot of ways. and you're right about the teaching thing, i want her to see me doing things that are good for my wellbeing, not just running from callout to callout and collapsing on the couch

Modelling self-care for your daughter is one of the most powerful things you can do. If she grows up watching her mum deliberately choose things that nourish her — the ocean, the cold, the breath, the pause — she learns that looking after yourself isn't selfish or indulgent, it's necessary. That's a lesson a lot of women don't get until they're already burnt out.

And the beach morning becomes something you share. Even when she's a teenager and thinks everything you do is embarrassing, she'll remember the winter mornings at the moana with her mum.

okay now i'm getting emotional about cold water therapy which is not what i expected from this conversation eh. but you're right, it's about more than the cold, it's about choosing to do something for myself and letting her see that. alright, mornings, once or twice a week to start, never after a callout, always with my friend, Maia on the beach. i'm doing this. winter usually feels like something to survive and this gives it something to look forward to. i'm genuinely excited

Turning winter from something to survive into something to look forward to — that's the whole point. And you're not doing this as some biohacking trend, you're doing it because the moana has always been your place and this is just going deeper into that relationship. The cold is the invitation to be fully present in a way that warm comfortable days don't demand of you. Enjoy that first dip. Breathe through the shock, feel the quiet afterwards, and let the ocean do what it's always done for you. Kia kaha.
